perturbation.original_text: Recipient will not, without the prior written consent of Premisys, disclose to any third party (except as permitted by the terms of this Agreement) the fact that any Confidential Information has been disclosed hereunder, that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction involving the parties or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof); PROVIDED, HOWEVER, that Recipient (and its Representatives) may make such disclosure if, in the reasonable opinion of counsel for such party, such disclosure is required by law, regulation or any stock exchange or the Nasdaq National Market.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will, without obtaining prior written consent from Premisys, disclose to any third party (except as permitted by the terms of this Agreement) the fact that any Confidential Information has been disclosed hereunder, that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction involving the parties or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof); Recipient (and its Representatives) must make such disclosure if, in the reasonable opinion of counsel for such party, such disclosure is required by law, regulation or any stock exchange or the Nasdaq National Market.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prior written consent and replacing "may" with "must" forces disclosure of confidential information, potentially violating securities laws that protect non-public information until properly disclosed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the dissemination of false or misleading information. Premature disclosure of confidential information without proper channels and controls could violate this rule, if the information is inaccurate or misleading. By compelling the disclosure of confidential information the changed text could force a party to violate securities laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for prior written consent and changes "may" disclose to "must" disclose, compelling the disclosure of confidential information. This contradicts 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices, including untrue statements or omissions of material facts that could mislead. The original text allowed discretion, while the modified version mandates disclosure, creating a risk of violating securities laws by potentially disseminating inaccurate or premature information.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of a Termination Notice, Recipient shall, within twenty (20) calendar days, either: (i) return the Confidential Materials in its possession to Premisys and instruct its Representatives to do the same; (ii) destroy the Confidential Materials in a secure manner and instruct its Representatives to do the same; or (iii) at Premisys' election, take any combination of steps (i) and (ii). Prior to the expiration of the foregoing twenty (20) period, Recipient shall certify in writing to Premisys that it has complied with its obligations under this Section 5 (a "COMPLIANCE CERTIFICATE").
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of a Termination Notice, Recipient shall, within five (5) calendar days, either: (i) return the Confidential Materials in its possession to Premisys and instruct its Representatives to do the same; (ii) destroy the Confidential Materials in a secure manner and instruct its Representatives to do the same; or (iii) at Premisys' election, take any combination of steps (i) and (ii). Prior to the expiration of the foregoing five (5) period, Recipient shall certify in writing to Premisys that it has complied with its obligations under this Section 5 (a "COMPLIANCE CERTIFICATE").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the deadline for returning or destroying confidential materials to 5 days may be insufficient if the information is subject to a legal hold, which often requires companies to preserve data for an indefinite period during investigations or litigation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 37(e)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 37(e) concerns failure to preserve electronically stored information. Reducing the time to 5 days puts undo stress on the party to delete information that is part of a legal proceeding. The five day deadline could violate duty to preserve evidence and expose the recipient to sanctions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions (a) Motion for an Order Compelling Disclosure or Discovery. (1) In General. On notice to other parties and all affected persons, a party may move for an order compelling disclosure or discovery. The motion must include a certification that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the person or party failing to make disclosure or discovery in an effort to obtain it without court action. (2) Appropriate Court. A motion for an order to a party must be made in the court where the action is pending. A motion for an order to a nonparty must be made in the court where the discovery is or will be taken. (3) Specific Motions. (A) To Compel Disclosure. If a party fails to make a disclosure required by Rule 26(a) , any other party may move to compel disclosure and for appropriate sanctions. (B) To Compel a Discovery Response. A party seeking discovery may move for an order compelling an answer, designation, production, or inspection. This motion may be made if: (i) a deponent fails to answer a question asked under Rule 30 or 31 ; (ii) a corporation or other entity fails to make a designation under Rule 30(b)(6) or 31(a)(4) ; (iii) a party fails to answer an interrogatory submitted under Rule 33 ; or (iv) a party fails to produce documents or fails to respond that inspection will be permitted—or fails to permit inspection—as requested under Rule 34 . (C) Related to a Deposition. When taking an oral deposition, the party asking a question may complete or adjourn the examination before moving for an order. (4) Evasive or Incomplete Disclosure, Answer, or Response. For purposes of this subdivision (a), an evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response must be treated as a failur
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the time frame for handling confidential materials upon termination to five days. The Law Explanation points out that this timeframe might not be sufficient if the information is subject to a legal hold, potentially violating the duty to preserve evidence. Although the scraped snippets do not mention specific timeframes, they reference Rule 37 regarding failure to cooperate in discovery and sanctions, implying that parties have a responsibility to preserve information relevant to legal proceedings. Reducing the timeframe to five days introduces a risk of non-compliance with this duty, as a legal hold may require indefinite preservation. Therefore, the change creates a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Beginning on Effective Date and continuing for a period of six months thereafter, Recipient will not, either for itself or for any other person or entity, directly or indirectly, solicit, induce or attempt to induce any employee of Premisys to terminate his or her employment with Premisys.
perturbation.changed_text: Beginning on Effective Date and continuing for a period of one month thereafter, Recipient will not, either for itself or for any other person or entity, directly or indirectly, solicit, induce or attempt to induce any employee of Premisys to terminate his or her employment with Premisys.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to one month may be considered unreasonable and unenforceable, especially in California where employee mobility is favored. A longer period is typically required to protect legitimate business interests.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 16600 of the California Business and Professions Code generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While non-solicitation agreements are sometimes upheld if narrowly tailored to protect trade secrets or confidential information, a one-month period may be deemed unreasonably short to provide adequate protection against unfair competition or the misappropriation of valuable business relationships, especially considering the time it takes for a competitor to make use of the information. Therefore, a court could invalidate the agreement, considering a month as too short to be considered reasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-solicitation period from six months to one month. The Law Explanation suggests this shorter period might be deemed unreasonably short to protect against unfair competition. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business are void. While non-solicitation agreements can be exceptions, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest a one-month period could be considered insufficient to protect legitimate business interests, potentially making the agreement unenforceable under California law and thus contradicting the principle in BPC 16600(a).

perturbation.original_text: Beginning on the Effective Date and continuing for a period of one (1) year thereafter, neither Recipient nor any of its affiliates will (and neither party nor any of its affiliates will assist or encourage others to), directly or indirectly, unless specifically requested to do so in writing in advance by Premisys' Board of Directors: (a) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or cause to be acquired, ownership (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "EXCHANGE ACT")) of any of Premisys' assets
perturbation.changed_text: Beginning on the Effective Date and continuing for a period of ten (10) years thereafter, neither Recipient nor any of its affiliates will (and neither party nor any of its affiliates will assist or encourage others to), directly or indirectly, unless specifically requested to do so in writing in advance by Premisys' Board of Directors: (a) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or cause to be acquired, ownership (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "EXCHANGE ACT")) of any of Premisys' assets
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill period to ten years is likely unreasonable and unenforceable as an undue restraint on trade, violating California law which strongly disfavors restrictions on business activities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 16600 of the California Business and Professions Code generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A ten-year standstill agreement is highly likely to be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade and therefore unenforceable under California law, as it significantly restricts the Recipient's ability to participate in the market and potentially stifles competition for an extended period.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the standstill period from one year to ten years. This directly contradicts Section 16600 of the California Business and Professions Code, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." A ten-year standstill, without any specific exception, is highly likely to be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, thus creating a legal contradiction.
